statement.markdown:
Given an array of integers, sort it in non‑decreasing order using the Bubble Sort technique. Bubble Sort repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. The process is repeated until the list is sorted. Output the sorted array.

You must read the array size first, then the array elements, and finally print the sorted sequence on one line.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer N, the number of elements in the array.
The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
statement.outputFormat: Print the sorted array in non‑decreasing order as N space‑separated integers on a single line.
statement.notes: Although any correct sorting algorithm would produce the right result, the intended solution demonstrates the Bubble Sort process.

constraints.description: Number of elements in the array.

examples.explanation: After applying Bubble Sort, the array becomes [1, 2, 4, 5, 8].
Sorting the three numbers results in [-5, -1, 0].

hints.text: Understand the Bubble Sort algorithm and how it works.
Read the size of the array using the N variable.
Read the array elements using the Ai variable.
Implement the Bubble Sort algorithm to sort the array.
Print the sorted array on one line.

editorial.title: Optimizing Bubble Sort: From Quadratic to Early‑Exit
editorial.intuition: Bubble sort repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are out of order. The naive implementation always performs n‑1 passes over the array, regardless of whether the array becomes sorted before the final pass. This guarantees correctness but leads to O(n²) time even for nearly sorted inputs. The key observation is that if a full pass completes without any swaps, the array is already sorted and further passes are unnecessary. By detecting this situation and terminating early, we preserve correctness while potentially reducing the number of comparisons dramatically for many practical cases.
editorial.bruteForce: The straightforward bubble sort runs two nested loops: the outer loop iterates n times, and the inner loop compares each adjacent pair up to n‑i‑1. Whenever arr[j] > arr[j+1] a swap occurs. This guarantees that after each outer iteration the largest unsorted element moves to its final position. However, the algorithm still performs all n passes even if the array becomes sorted early, resulting in a worst‑case time complexity of O(n²) and constant extra space.
editorial.betterSolution: Introduce a flag that records whether any swap happened during a pass. If after a complete inner loop the flag remains false, the array is sorted and the algorithm can terminate immediately. This simple optimization keeps the same O(n²) worst‑case time but can reduce the average time to O(n) for already sorted or nearly sorted arrays.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimized algorithm runs as follows: for i from 0 to n-1, set swapped=false; for j from 0 to n-i-2, compare arr[j] and arr[j+1]; if arr[j] > arr[j+1] swap them and set swapped=true. After the inner loop, if swapped is false break. This preserves the correctness of bubble sort while adding an early‑exit condition. The algorithm uses O(1) additional space and runs in O(n²) time in the worst case, but in practice it often behaves like O(n).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Bubble sort’s correctness relies on the fact that each pass moves the largest unsorted element to its correct position. The early‑exit flag does not alter this property; it merely stops the algorithm when a pass makes no swaps, which can only happen when every adjacent pair is already in order. Therefore, the array is sorted at that point, and terminating is safe. Since the algorithm still performs all necessary swaps when needed, it remains correct for all inputs.
editorial.dryRun: Consider arr=[3,2,1]. Pass 1: compare 3>2 swap→[2,3,1]; compare 3>1 swap→[2,1,3]; swapped=true. Pass 2: compare 2>1 swap→[1,2,3]; swapped=true. Pass 3: compare 1>2? no swap; swapped=false → break. Result is sorted [1,2,3].
commonMistakes: Using a fixed number of passes instead of n‑i‑1, causing unnecessary comparisons.
Failing to reset the swapped flag at the start of each outer loop.
Assuming the algorithm always needs n passes and ignoring the early‑exit condition.
Not handling the case when n=0 or n=1, leading to index errors.
Using a non‑constant extra space (e.g., creating new lists) instead of in‑place swaps.
Misinterpreting the flag logic, e.g., breaking when swapped==true instead of false.
